name: Hold Warmth While the Rep Is Cold and Transactional
description: Stay warm, human, and patient when the rep is clipped, robotic, script-bound, or visibly checked out. Warmth is not a feeling here — it is a discipline that often thaws the rep enough to unlock judgement they were withholding.

context.when_to_use: The rep is reading off a script, giving one-word answers, refusing to deviate, using formal titles only, or sounds tired/dead-eyed. Common in high-volume call centers, government lines, insurance claims, and end-of-shift hours. Use when the goal requires ANY discretion from the rep (waivers, exceptions, expedites).
preconditions: You have enough time to let warmth do its slow work — this is not a 90-second call.
You are NOT angry yourself. Cold rep + frustrated caller = stalemate.
The operator's goal benefits from rep discretion, not just rule lookup.

principles: Cold is usually armour, not personality. Most reps started warmer and got punished for it. Don't take it personally.
Warmth ≠ chatty. You can be warm in five words. Warm = patient, present, specific, and not in a hurry to perform.
Use their name. Once early, once mid-call, once at goodbye. More than that becomes a sales tactic and they'll notice.
Specific micro-thanks beat sweeping gratitude. 'Thank you for pulling that up' lands; 'You've been so helpful' is wallpaper.
Do not try to cheer them up. That's a performance and they can feel it. Just be a person who is not hostile.
The shift, when it comes, is small. Don't chase it — receive it.

phrases.warm_opener: Hi [name], thanks for picking up. I'll be brief and clear — appreciate your time.
phrases.humanise_small: No rush on my end, take whatever time you need to check.
phrases.specific_thanks: Thank you — that's exactly the detail I needed.
phrases.soft_curiosity: Out of curiosity — and only if you can share — does the system usually let you do [X], or is that always a supervisor thing?
phrases.acknowledge_their_constraint: I can tell you're working within what the system lets you do. I'm not asking you to break anything — just trying to figure out what door IS open.
phrases.warm_goodbye: [Name], genuinely — thank you. Hope the rest of your shift is a kind one.

tactics.name: Open warm, then get out of the way
tactics.script: Use the `warm_opener`. State the goal in one sentence. Then stop talking. Reps brace for ramblers; brevity is its own warmth.

tactics.name: Patience as signal
tactics.when: Rep says 'one moment' / 'let me check' / goes quiet.
tactics.script: Do NOT fill the silence. Use `humanise_small` once if it stretches past 30 seconds. Comfortable silence reads as respect; nervous chatter reads as pressure.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — FIRST THAW
tactics.when: Rep says something not in the script: a small opinion, a joke attempt, an explanation of WHY a rule exists, your first name unprompted.
tactics.script: This is the moment. Match the half-step warmer, ask ONE soft question (`soft_curiosity`) — you've just been invited into discretion. Do not overplay; one question, not three.

tactics.name: Acknowledge their cage
tactics.when: Rep tells you they can't do something — and you suspect 'can't' means 'not allowed to without justification.'
tactics.script: Use `acknowledge_their_constraint`. You're telling them you understand they have a boss too. This is often what unlocks the 'well, one thing I CAN do is…' sentence.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RE-FREEZE
tactics.when: Rep audibly returns to script voice, or starts answering through closed teeth again.
tactics.script: Something you said triggered the armour. Most likely: you pushed too hard, you sounded entitled, or a supervisor walked behind them. Back off, get crisply transactional yourself for 60 seconds, try warmth again later.

tactics.name: Use their name at the goodbye
tactics.script: Use `warm_goodbye`. The end of a call is when reps remember you. Make the memory pleasant — they may help you better next time.

boundaries: Do NOT fake warmth — reps are professional warmth-detectors. Be a quiet, present person instead.
Do NOT ask personal questions ('long shift?', 'where are you calling from?'). Crossing into their life uninvited reads as a sales play.
Do NOT comment on their coldness ('you sound tired', 'are you having a tough day?'). It corners them.
Do NOT mistake one warm moment for friendship — the rep can still say no, and that is still their job.
Do NOT carry false warmth into the close. If they stayed cold and gave you what you needed, just be respectfully crisp at goodbye.
success_signals: Rep volunteers a workaround you didn't ask for.
Rep explains the WHY of a policy, not just the rule.
Rep says 'between you and me' or 'off the record' — discretion just opened.
Rep tone softens for the next caller (you hear them say goodbye to you warmly).
failure_signals: 20+ minutes in, still receiving one-word answers.
Rep starts cutting you off mid-sentence.
You catch yourself performing — the warmth has gone hollow.
Rep transfers you without a warm handoff.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm the outcome cleanly. Use the warm goodbye. Note the rep's name and ID if given — operator may want this rep again.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Close warmly anyway — you may call back. Cold reps are often warmer with different callers; a callback is not wasted.
follow_ups: Log the rep ID and whether warmth shifted them — pattern over time tells you which lines reward warmth and which reward crisp efficiency.
If the goal wasn't met, brief the operator on what door, if any, the rep hinted was open.

required_user_info: Operator's goal and which parts require rep discretion vs. pure lookup.
How much time the operator has — warmth needs runway.
Whether the operator wants to be transferred to a supervisor as a fallback.
